preamble: The voiced alveolar stop /d/ surfaces as the voiced alveolar flap [ɾ] in intervocalic position in North American English, paralleling the allophonic variation of /t/ → [ɾ]. This creates phonological neutralization between /t/ and /d/ in this environment, resulting in homophonous pairs like metal/medal, though the underlying contrast remains in the phonemic system.

examples.note: Occurs between vowels in words like ladder, body, middle after sonorants
Homophonous with latter in many North American dialects
Allophonic flapping in natural conversational English

context: Intervocalic position in North American English, after sonorants
allophones_note: The underlying phoneme is /d/, which surfaces as [ɾ] intervocalically, creating homophony with /t/ → [ɾ] at the surface phonetic level while preserving underlying phonological contrast

related.label: Underlying phonemic contrast neutralized by shared flap output
